year_label: 1 f.Kr. / 1 e.Kr. (fyrirframreiknað gregorískt tímatal; ár 1 samkvæmt hefðbundnu kristnu tímatali)
era_context: Síðhelleniski heimurinn færist yfir í rómverska keisaratímabilið. Ágústus (Oktavíanus) hefur treyst völd sín eftir borgarastyrjaldirnar og ríkir sem fyrsti rómverski keisarinn. Í austanverðu Miðjarðarhafi halda hellenisk konungsríki velli undir rómverskum yfirráðum. Í Kína er Han-veldið á blómaskeiði sínu undir stjórn Xuan keisara. Á Indlandi er Márya-veldið löngu hrunið og svæðisbundin ríki ráða ríkjum. Í Mið-Ameríku er klassísk menning Maja á mótunarskeiði.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Afríka sunnan Sahara (takmarkaðar skriflegar heimildir; fornleifaskráning ófullkomin)
Eyjaálfa (engar skriflegar heimildir; tímasetning fornleifa óviss)

events.title: Ágústus treystir Pax Romana; Rómaveldi nær mestu landfræðilegu útbreiðslu undir stjórn hans
events.description: Um árið 0/1 e.Kr. hefur Ágústus (Oktavíanus) lokið umbreytingu rómverska lýðveldisins í einveldi í reynd, þótt hann viðhaldi formi lýðveldisins. Hann ber titlana Princeps (fyrsti borgarinn) og Imperator. Veldið teygir sig frá Spáni til Sýrlands, frá Rín til Egyptalands. Þetta ár fellur innan Pax Romana, tímabils tiltölulegs friðar og stöðugleika sem mun vara í um tvær aldir. Valdatíð Ágústusar markar upphaf eiginlegs keisaratímabils og leggur grunn að stjórnsýslukerfum sem munu einkenna Róm um aldir.

sources.name: Res Gestae Divi Augusti (Afrek hins guðdómlega Ágústusar)

events.certainty_note: Valdatíð Ágústusar og stjórnmálaskipan Rómaveldis á þessu tímabili eru ítarlega skráð í samtímaáritunum, myntum og síðari sagnaritun.

events.title: Han-veldið undir stjórn Xuan keisara; Kína á hátindi landfræðilegs og menningarlegs máttar
events.description: Xuan keisari af Han-ætt (r. 74–49 f.Kr.) er nýlega látinn; eftirmaður hans er Yuan keisari (r. 49–33 f.Kr.), þótt sumar heimildir telji valdatökuna hafa orðið örlítið fyrr. Han-veldið er á blómaskeiði sínu og hefur aukið yfirráð Kínverja til vesturs meðfram Silkiveginum og treyst stjórnsýslukerfi sín. Veldið er blómlegt, með háþróað embættismannakerfi byggt á gildum konfúsíusarstefnu og verðleikaprófum. Á þessu tímabili verða miklar framfarir í menningu og tækni, þar á meðal í stjörnufræði, stærðfræði og listum.
key_figures: Yuan keisari af Han-ætt (r. 49–33 f.Kr., eða hugsanlega fyrr)

sources.name: Shiji (Skýrslur hins mikla sagnaritara) eftir Sima Qian, fram haldið af Sima Tan
Han Shu (Bók Han-ættarinnar) eftir Ban Gu
Fornleifafræðileg gögn úr Han-gröfum og áletranir

events.certainty_note: Stjórnmálaskipan Han-veldisins og keisarar þess eru vel skráð í kínverskum sagnfræðiheimildum og fornleifagögnum.

events.title: Parþaveldið stjórnar Silkiveginum; stórveldi í Vestur-Asíu
events.description: Parþaveldið, undir stjórn Arsakída-ættarinnar, drottnar yfir svæðinu milli Rómaveldis og Han-Kína. Orodes II konungur (r. um 57–37 f.Kr.) eða eftirmaður hans ræður yfir víðáttumiklum svæðum og þjónar sem mikilvægur milliliður í viðskiptum milli austurs og vesturs. Parþar eru þekktir fyrir hernaðarmátt sinn, sérstaklega riddara með boga, og yfirráð þeirra yfir verslunarleiðum veita þeim veruleg efnahagsleg og stjórnmálaleg völd. Á þessu tímabili á sér stað styrking á stjórnsýslukerfum Parþa.
key_figures: Orodes II (r. um 57–37 f.Kr.) eða eftirmaður
events.certainty_note: Tímatal Parþa er ekki eins nákvæmlega skráð og heimildir Rómverja eða Han-verja. Tímasetning einstakra þjóðhöfðingja er áætluð, með óvissu upp á ±2–3 ár.

events.title: Upphaf kristinna samfélaga í Júdeu og Austurlöndum nær
events.description: Á því ári sem jafnan er nefnt 1 e.Kr. (eða 1 AD í kristnu tímatali), telja snemma kristnar heimildir að Jesús frá Nasaret hafi fæðst, þótt nútímafræði tímasetji það á milli 6–4 f.Kr. Óháð nákvæmri dagsetningu markar þetta tímabil upphaf kristinna samfélaga í Júdeu og Austurlöndum nær. Þessi samfélög eru í upphafi gyðingleg í eðli sínu og líta á Jesú sem Messías innan ramma gyðingdóms. Hreyfingin er enn mjög lítil og staðbundin; hún verður ekki að mikilvægu sögulegu afli fyrr en mörgum öldum síðar. Árið 0/1 e.Kr. hefur táknræna þýðingu í kristinni hefð en sögulega séð táknar það snemmbúið og lítt þekkt skeið þess sem síðar varð kristni.
key_figures: Jesús frá Nasaret (hefðbundin fæðingardagsetning, þó líklega 6–4 f.Kr.)
Lærisveinar og postular (Pétur, Jakob, Jóhannes o.fl.)

sources.name: Guðspjöllin (Matteus, Markús, Lúkas, Jóhannes) — skrifuð 70–100 e.Kr.

events.certainty_note: Fæðing Jesú er jafnan miðuð við árið 1 e.Kr., en nútímafræði staðsetur hana 6–4 f.Kr. byggt á dauða Heródesar (4 f.Kr.) og manntali Kvíriníusar (6 e.Kr.). Engar samtímaheimildir skrá fæðingu Jesú. Guðspjöllin voru skrifuð 40–70 árum eftir þá atburði sem þau lýsa. Þessi færsla er tekin með vegna menningarlegrar og sögulegrar þýðingar, ekki sem staðfest söguleg staðreynd.

events.title: Júdea undir rómverskri stjórn; ríki Heródesar skiptist á milli sona hans
events.description: Eftir dauða Heródesar mikla árið 4 f.Kr. var ríki hans skipt á milli sona hans með rómverskri tilskipun. Heródes Antípas stjórnar Galíleu og Pereu sem fjórðungsstjóri; Heródes Filippus stjórnar norðausturhlutanum; Arkelaos stjórnar eiginlegri Júdeu en er vikið frá völdum árið 6 e.Kr. og rómverskir landstjórar taka við. Júdea er undir vaxandi rómverskri stjórnsýslu og skattlagningu. Á þessu tímabili gætir vaxandi óánægju Gyðinga með rómversk yfirráð og ýmsar andspyrnuhreyfingar og heimsendasöfnuðir koma fram. Svæðið er efnahagslega nýtt og pólitískt óstöðugt.
key_figures: Heródes Antípas (fjórðungsstjóri í Galíleu, r. 4 f.Kr.–39 e.Kr.)
Heródes Filippus (fjórðungsstjóri í norðaustri, r. 4 f.Kr.–34 e.Kr.)
Arkelaos (þjóðhöfðingi í Júdeu, r. 4 f.Kr.–6 e.Kr.)

sources.name: Jósefus, Fornsögur Gyðinga og Stríð Gyðinga

events.certainty_note: Skipting ríkis Heródesar og skipun sona hans eru vel skráð hjá Jósefusi og staðfest með myntum og áletrunum.

events.title: Mótunarskeið menningar Maja; snemmbúnar miðstöðvar trúarathafna í Mið-Ameríku
events.description: Menning Maja er á mótunarskeiði (fyrirklassíska tímabilinu) og færist í átt að klassíska tímabilinu (250–900 e.Kr.). Snemmbúnar miðstöðvar trúarathafna og byggðir eru í mótun á Júkatanskaga og í Mið-Ameríku. Landbúnaður byggist á maísrækt og stéttaskipting verður greinilegri. Ritkerfi eru farin að þróast, þótt þau séu ekki enn fullmótuð. Á þessu tímabili koma fram trúarleg og stjörnufræðileg þekkingarkerfi sem munu einkenna klassíska menningu Maja.

sources.name: Fornleifafræðileg gögn frá stöðum eins og Nakbé, El Mirador og Cerros

events.certainty_note: Tímatal Maja á mótunarskeiðinu byggist á lögfræði fornleifa og geislakolsmælingum, með óvissu upp á ±50–100 ár. Sértækir atburðir eru ekki skráðir; þessi færsla lýsir víðara menningarlegu samhengi.

disconfirming_evidence: Hefðbundin kristin tímasetning á fæðingu Jesú við árið 1 e.Kr. (eða 1 AD) er dregin í efa af nútímafræðimönnum. Flestir fræðimenn staðsetja fæðinguna á milli 6–4 f.Kr., byggt á dauða Heródesar mikla árið 4 f.Kr. og manntali Kvíriníusar árið 6 e.Kr. Guðspjöllin gefa misvísandi frásagnir af fæðingunni og voru skrifuð áratugum eftir atburðina. Engar samtímaheimildir utan kristninnar skrá fæðingu Jesú. Árið 0/1 e.Kr. hefur því táknræna þýðingu í kristinni hefð en samsvarar ekki staðfestum sögulegum atburði. Auk þess er 'Pax Romana' undir Ágústusi stundum rómantíseruð; þetta tímabil fól í sér umfangsmiklar herferðir, bælingu uppreisna og styrkingu valda með valdi.
historiographic_note: Árið 0/1 e.Kr. er einstaklega vel skráð hvað varðar rómverska og kínverska (Han) heima, með ríkulegum frumheimildum, myntum, áletrunum og fornleifagögnum. Hins vegar er sögulega skráningin mjög hliðholl yfirstéttum og læsum siðmenningum (Róm, Han-Kína, Parþía). Afríka sunnan Sahara, Norður-Evrópa og stór hluti Ameríku eru nánast fjarverandi í skrifuðum heimildum. Sú ákvörðun kristinnar hefðar að miða árið 1 e.Kr. við fæðingu Jesú er eftir á búin til (gregoríska tímatalið var ekki tekið upp fyrr en á 16. öld e.Kr.). Fyrir svæði utan hins vestræna heims eru fornleifagögn brotakennd og háð óvissu í tímasetningu. Heimildirnar sem varðveist hafa endurspegla hlutdrægni læsra yfirstétta og síðari sagnaritara; reynsla bænda, þræla og ólæsra þjóða er að mestu ósýnileg.

graph_edges.note: Valdatíð Ágústusar lýkur árið 14 e.Kr.; Tíberíus tekur við af honum og heldur keisarakerfinu áfram.
Blómaskeið Han-veldisins heldur áfram frá fyrri tímabilum.
Snemma kristin samfélög leiða að lokum til krossfestingar Jesú (hefðbundin dagsetning um 30 e.Kr.) og útbreiðslu kristni.
Arkelaosi er vikið frá völdum árið 6 e.Kr.; Júdea kemur undir beina stjórn rómverskra landstjóra.

era_context: Síðhelleniski heimurinn er áfram undir yfirráðum Rómverja. Ágústus treystir völd sín sem fyrsti rómverski keisarinn (valdatími 27 f.Kr.–14 e.Kr.). Han-veldið er á blómaskeiði sínu í Kína. Parþaveldið stjórnar Silkiveginum. Í Júdeu er Heródes mikli enn á lífi og við völd (d. 4 f.Kr.). Í Mið-Ameríku eru fyrstu byggðir Maja í mótun.

events.title: Valdatími Ágústusar heldur áfram; samþjöppun rómversks keisaravalds
events.description: Ágústus heldur áfram valdatíð sinni sem fyrsti rómverski keisarinn eftir að hafa treyst völd sín í kjölfar borgarastyrjalda síðari hluta lýðveldistímans. Um 2 f.Kr. hefur hann komið á fót grunnstoðum keisarakerfisins, þar á meðal pretóríusvarðliðinu, embættismannakerfinu og stjórnsýslu skattlanda. Hann viðheldur yfirskini lýðveldisstjórnar á meðan hann fer með æðsta vald. Þetta ár fellur innan Pax Romana, tímabils hlutfallslegs friðar og stöðugleika. Ágústus er tekinn að eldast (hann fæddist 63 f.Kr. og er því 61 árs árið 2 f.Kr.) og er byrjaður að undirbúa eftirmenn sína.
events.certainty_note: Valdatími og gjörðir Ágústusar eru ítarlega skráðar.

events.title: Han-veldið heldur áfram undir stjórn Yuan keisara; verslun á Silkiveginum eykst
events.description: Han-veldið undir stjórn Yuan keisara (valdatími 49–33 f.Kr.) heldur áfram að stækka og treysta yfirráð sín yfir verslunarleiðum til vesturs. Silkivegurinn verður sífellt mikilvægari tengiliður fyrir verslun milli Kína og Miðjarðarhafssvæðisins, þótt bein samskipti séu enn takmörkuð. Kínverskir kaupmenn og sendiboðar ferðast vestur á bóginn og framandi vörur byrja að berast til Kína frá Mið-Asíu og víðar. Þessi verslun færir ekki aðeins efnislegan varning heldur einnig hugmyndir, tækni og menningarleg áhrif.

sources.name: Fornleifafræðileg gögn frá stöðum við Silkiveginn

events.certainty_note: Útþensla verslunar Han-veldisins er skráð í kínverskum sagnfræðiritum og staðfest með fornleifarannsóknum á verslunarvörum.

events.title: Heródes mikli ríkir í Júdeu; byggingarframkvæmdir og menningarleg þróun
events.description: Heródes mikli (valdatími 37–4 f.Kr.) er á síðustu árum valdatíðar sinnar. Hann er þekktur fyrir metnaðarfullar byggingarframkvæmdir, þar á meðal stækkun og endurbætur á öðru musterinu í Jerúsalem, byggingu Masada og stofnun Caesarea Maritima. Þessi verkefni endurspegla bæði metnað hans til að láta eftir sig varanlega arfleifð og löngun hans til að samþætta Júdeu inn í helleníska heiminn. Hins vegar einkennist valdatími hans einnig af innri deilum, ofsóknaræði og aftökum á fjölskyldumeðlimum. Hann er aldraður og heilsu hans hrakar; ríkiserfðir eru óvissar.
key_figures: Synir hans (Heródes Antípas, Heródes Filippus, Arkelaos)

sources.name: Jósefus, Fornsögur Gyðinga og Stríð Gyðinga

events.certainty_note: Valdatími Heródesar og byggingarverkefni eru vel skráð hjá Jósefusi og staðfest með umfangsmiklum fornleifarannsóknum.

events.title: Parþaveldið undir stjórn Óródesar II; hernaðarleg og diplómatísk samþjöppun
events.description: Parþaveldið undir stjórn Óródesar II konungs (valdatími u.þ.b. 57–37 f.Kr.) eða eftirmanns hans heldur áfram að treysta völd sín og viðhalda stjórn á Silkiveginum. Parþar eiga í áframhaldandi átökum við Róm um yfirráð í Armeníu og Mesópótamíu. Hernaðarmáttur þeirra, sérstaklega riddaralið með boga, gerir þá að ógnvænlegum andstæðingi gegn útþenslu Rómverja. Hirð Parþa er fáguð og heimsborgaraleg, þar sem persneskum, grískum og mið-asískum áhrifum ægir saman.
events.certainty_note: Tímatal Parþa er ekki jafn nákvæmlega skráð og rómverskar heimildir; tímasetningar eru áætlaðar.

events.title: Fyrstu byggðir og helgiathafnastöðvar Maja í Mið-Ameríku
events.description: Menning Maja heldur áfram þróun sinni á mótunartímabilinu. Snemma helgiathafnastöðvar eins og Nakbé og El Mirador stækka og verða flóknari. Landbúnaðarhættir verða þróaðri með tilkomu áveitukerfa og stalla. Félagslegt stigveldi verður skýrara, með vísbendingum um yfirstéttir og sérhæft handverksfólk. Grunnurinn að klassískri menningu Maja er lagður.

sources.name: Fornleifafræðileg gögn frá stöðum við Maja

events.certainty_note: Tímatal Maja á þessu tímabili byggist á fornleifafræðilegum gögnum með óvissu upp á ±50–100 ár.

disconfirming_evidence: Engar sérstakar mótsagnakenndar vísbendingar hafa fundist varðandi skráða atburði. Hins vegar er 'Pax Romana' stundum ofmetinn; á þessu tímabili áttu sér stað áframhaldandi herferðir og bæling uppreisna, sérstaklega í skattlöndunum. Byggingarverkefni Heródesar mikla voru glæsileg en fjármögnuð með þungri skattlagningu og voru ekki óumdeild meðal íbúa Júdeu.
historiographic_note: Árið 2 f.Kr. er vel skráð hvað varðar Rómaveldi og Han-veldið. Sögulegar heimildir hallast mjög að yfirstéttum og læsum siðmenningum. Afríka sunnan Sahara, Norður-Evrópa og stór hluti Ameríku eru nánast fjarverandi í rituðum heimildum. Fyrir svæði utan hins vestræna heims eru fornleifafræðileg gögn brotakennd og háð óvissu í tímasetningum.

graph_edges.note: Valdatími Ágústusar heldur áfram yfir bæði árin.
Dauði Heródesar árið 4 f.Kr. leiðir til skiptingar ríkis hans á milli sona hans.

era_context: Augustus consolidates Roman Imperial power. The Han Dynasty is in its mature phase. The Parthian Empire controls Central Asia and the Silk Road. Herod the Great rules Judea. Early Maya civilization is developing in Mesoamerica.

events.title: Augustus' reign continues; aging emperor and succession concerns
events.description: Augustus continues his reign as the first Roman Emperor. By 3 BCE, he is 60 years old and increasingly concerned with succession. His preferred heir, Marcellus, died in 23 BCE; his general Agrippa died in 12 BCE. Augustus has now adopted Tiberius as his heir, though this is a reluctant choice. The empire is stable and prosperous, with the Pax Romana continuing. Augustus is working to consolidate the succession and ensure the stability of the Imperial system after his death.
key_figures: Various provincial governors and military commanders

sources.name: Suetonius, Life of Augustus and Life of Tiberius

events.certainty_note: Augustus' reign and succession arrangements are well-documented.

events.title: Han Dynasty continues; cultural and technological achievements
events.description: The Han Dynasty under Emperor Yuan continues to achieve cultural and technological advances. This period sees developments in astronomy, mathematics, medicine, and the arts. The Han court is sophisticated and cosmopolitan, with scholars and artists enjoying imperial patronage. The examination system for civil service is becoming more formalized, based on Confucian principles. The empire is economically prosperous, with agriculture and trade flourishing.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Han tombs and artifacts

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty's cultural achievements are documented in historical records and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Herod the Great's final years; internal strife and succession uncertainty
events.description: Herod the Great is in the final years of his reign (he will die in 4 BCE). His health is declining, and his court is rife with intrigue and suspicion. He has executed several family members, including his wife Mariamne and some of his sons, due to paranoia about succession. His remaining sons are jockeying for position, and there is uncertainty about who will succeed him. The Jewish population is restless under his rule, and there are signs of resistance and rebellion. His death will lead to the division of his kingdom among his surviving sons.
key_figures: His sons (Herod Antipas, Herod Philip, Archelaus)
Jewish religious leaders and resistance movements

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews and Jewish War

events.certainty_note: Herod's final years and succession concerns are well-documented in Josephus.

events.title: Parthian Empire maintains control of Silk Road; ongoing Roman-Parthian tensions
events.description: The Parthian Empire continues to maintain control over the Silk Road and Central Asia. There are ongoing tensions with Rome over control of Armenia and Mesopotamia, though no major military campaigns are recorded for this specific year. The Parthians are consolidating their power and maintaining their position as a major world power, rivaling Rome in the west and China in the east.
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is less precisely documented; dating is approximate.

events.title: Maya civilization in Formative period; development of early writing systems
events.description: Maya civilization continues its development in the Formative period. Early writing systems are beginning to emerge, though they are not yet fully standardized. Ceremonial centers are growing in size and complexity. The foundations of the astronomical and mathematical knowledge systems that will characterize Classic Maya civilization are being laid. Agriculture is becoming more sophisticated, with the development of irrigation and terracing.
events.certainty_note: Maya chronology in this period is based on archaeological evidence with uncertainty of ±50–100 years.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the extent of Herod's paranoia and the number of family members he executed are sometimes exaggerated in later sources; Josephus may have embellished these accounts for dramatic effect.
historiographic_note: The year 3 BCE is well-documented for the Roman and Han worlds. The historical record is heavily skewed toward elite, literate civilizations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and much of the Americas are virtually absent from written records.

graph_edges.note: Augustus' reign continues.
Herod's death leads to the division of his kingdom.

era_context: Augustus consolidates Roman Imperial power. The Han Dynasty is in its mature phase. The Parthian Empire controls Central Asia. Herod the Great dies this year, leading to the division of his kingdom. Early Maya civilization is developing in Mesoamerica.

events.title: Death of Herod the Great; division of his kingdom among his sons
events.description: Herod the Great dies in 4 BCE after a reign of 37 years (37–4 BCE). His kingdom is divided among his surviving sons by Roman decree. Herod Antipas receives Galilee and Perea as tetrarch; Herod Philip receives the northeast (Batanea, Trachonitis, Auranitis) as tetrarch; Archelaus receives Judea, Samaria, and Idumea as ethnarch. This division marks the end of a unified Jewish kingdom under Herod and the beginning of a period of fragmentation and increasing Roman control. Archelaus' rule is unpopular and will lead to his deposition in 6 CE, after which Judea comes under direct Roman prefectural rule.
key_figures: Herod Antipas (tetrarch of Galilee, r. 4 BCE–39 CE)
Herod Philip (tetrarch of the northeast, r. 4 BCE–34 CE)
Archelaus (ethnarch of Judea, r. 4 BCE–6 CE)

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews and Jewish War

events.certainty_note: Herod's death and the division of his kingdom are well-documented in Josephus and confirmed by archaeological evidence. The date 4 BCE is established by Josephus' account of Herod's death and the subsequent events.

events.title: Augustus continues reign; Roman Empire stable and prosperous
events.description: Augustus continues his reign as the first Roman Emperor. The empire is stable and prosperous, with the Pax Romana continuing. Augustus is now 59 years old and has consolidated the succession with Tiberius as his adopted heir. The empire stretches from Spain to Syria, from the Rhine to Egypt. Augustus is working to ensure the stability of the Imperial system and the smooth transition of power after his death.
events.certainty_note: Augustus' reign is well-documented.

events.title: Han Dynasty under Emperor Yuan; continued prosperity and cultural achievement
events.description: The Han Dynasty under Emperor Yuan continues to achieve cultural and technological advances. The empire is economically prosperous, with agriculture and trade flourishing. The Silk Road trade is expanding, bringing exotic goods and ideas from Central Asia and beyond. The Han court is sophisticated and cosmopolitan, with scholars and artists enjoying imperial patronage.
events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty's prosperity and cultural achievements are documented in historical records and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Parthian Empire maintains power; control of Silk Road and Central Asia
events.description: The Parthian Empire continues to maintain control over the Silk Road and Central Asia. The Parthians are consolidating their power and maintaining their position as a major world power, rivaling Rome in the west and China in the east. There are ongoing tensions with Rome over control of Armenia and Mesopotamia, but no major military campaigns are recorded for this specific year.
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is less precisely documented; dating is approximate.

events.title: Maya civilization in Formative period; ceremonial centers and agricultural development
events.description: Maya civilization continues its development in the Formative period. Ceremonial centers are growing in size and complexity. Agricultural practices are becoming more sophisticated, with the development of irrigation systems and terracing. Early writing systems are emerging. The foundations of Classic Maya civilization are being laid.
events.certainty_note: Maya chronology in this period is based on archaeological evidence with uncertainty of ±50–100 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional Christian dating of Jesus' birth to 1 BCE or 1 CE is disputed by modern scholarship. Most scholars place the birth between 6–4 BCE, with 4 BCE being a plausible date given Herod's death in that year (Matthew's Gospel places Jesus' birth during Herod's reign). However, the Gospels provide conflicting accounts and were written decades after the events. No contemporary non-Christian sources document Jesus' birth. Additionally, some scholars argue that Herod's death may have occurred in 5 BCE rather than 4 BCE, based on astronomical calculations and other evidence, though 4 BCE remains the scholarly consensus.
historiographic_note: The year 4 BCE is well-documented for the Roman and Han worlds, particularly regarding Herod's death and the division of his kingdom. The historical record is heavily skewed toward elite, literate civilizations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and much of the Americas are virtually absent from written records. For non-Western regions, archaeological evidence is fragmentary and subject to dating uncertainties. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and later compilers.

graph_edges.note: The division of Herod's kingdom leads to the political situation described in year 0/1 CE.
Archelaus' unpopular rule leads to his deposition in 6 CE.
Augustus' reign continues.

era_context: Augustus consolidates Roman Imperial power. The Han Dynasty is in its mature phase. The Parthian Empire controls Central Asia. Herod the Great is in the final years of his reign in Judea. Early Maya civilization is developing in Mesoamerica.

events.title: Herod the Great in final years; internal strife and succession uncertainty
events.description: Herod the Great is in the final years of his reign (he will die in 4 BCE). His health is declining, and his court is rife with intrigue and suspicion. He has executed several family members due to paranoia about succession. His remaining sons are jockeying for position. The Jewish population is restless under his rule, and there are signs of resistance and rebellion. His death is imminent, and the succession is uncertain.
key_figures: His sons (Herod Antipas, Herod Philip, Archelaus)
Jewish religious leaders and resistance movements

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews and Jewish War

events.certainty_note: Herod's final years and succession concerns are well-documented in Josephus.

events.title: Augustus' reign continues; aging emperor and succession consolidation
events.description: Augustus continues his reign as the first Roman Emperor. The empire is stable and prosperous. Augustus is now 58 years old and has consolidated the succession with Tiberius as his adopted heir. The Pax Romana continues, with relative peace and stability throughout the empire. Augustus is working to ensure the smooth transition of power after his death.
events.certainty_note: Augustus' reign is well-documented.

events.title: Han Dynasty under Emperor Yuan; prosperity and cultural achievement
events.description: The Han Dynasty under Emperor Yuan continues to achieve cultural and technological advances. The empire is economically prosperous, with agriculture and trade flourishing. The Silk Road trade is expanding, bringing exotic goods and ideas from Central Asia and beyond. The Han court is sophisticated and cosmopolitan, with scholars and artists enjoying imperial patronage.
events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty's prosperity and cultural achievements are documented in historical records and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Parthian Empire maintains control of Silk Road and Central Asia
events.description: The Parthian Empire continues to maintain control over the Silk Road and Central Asia. The Parthians are consolidating their power and maintaining their position as a major world power. There are ongoing tensions with Rome over control of Armenia and Mesopotamia, but no major military campaigns are recorded for this specific year.
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is less precisely documented; dating is approximate.

events.title: Maya civilization in Formative period; development of ceremonial centers and agriculture
events.description: Maya civilization continues its development in the Formative period. Ceremonial centers are growing in size and complexity. Agricultural practices are becoming more sophisticated, with the development of irrigation systems and terracing. Early writing systems are emerging. The foundations of Classic Maya civilization are being laid.
events.certainty_note: Maya chronology in this period is based on archaeological evidence with uncertainty of ±50–100 years.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the extent of Herod's paranoia and the number of family members he executed are sometimes exaggerated in later sources; Josephus may have embellished these accounts for dramatic effect.
historiographic_note: The year 5 BCE is well-documented for the Roman and Han worlds. The historical record is heavily skewed toward elite, literate civilizations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and much of the Americas are virtually absent from written records. For non-Western regions, archaeological evidence is fragmentary and subject to dating uncertainties. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and later compilers.

graph_edges.note: Herod's death in 4 BCE leads to the division of his kingdom.
Augustus' reign continues.

era_context: The late Hellenistic world under Roman expansion. Augustus (Octavian) rules the Roman Empire in its early consolidation phase. In the Eastern Mediterranean, Hellenistic kingdoms persist under Roman suzerainty. In China, the Western Han dynasty approaches its final decades under Emperor Xuan. In India, the Satavahana dynasty expands in the Deccan. This is the era of the Pax Romana's establishment and the final flowering of Hellenistic culture before its absorption into Roman civilization.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record for this specific year)
Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records)
Mesoamerica (limited dating precision for this period)

events.title: Herod the Great's reign continues; construction projects in Judaea
events.description: Herod I (Herod the Great) continues his rule as client king of Judaea under Roman authority. This period saw ongoing construction projects, including work on the Second Temple in Jerusalem and fortifications. Herod's reign was marked by internal consolidation, suppression of dissent, and architectural patronage. His rule maintained stability in the region while serving Roman strategic interests in the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Herod's reign is well-attested (37–4 BCE), but specific events within individual years are often conflated or undated in sources. This year falls within his documented rule but lacks year-specific contemporary documentation.

events.title: Augustus consolidates power in Rome; administrative reforms
events.region: Roman Empire (Mediterranean, Western Europe, North Africa, Near East)
events.description: Augustus (Octavian) continues administrative consolidation of the Roman Empire. By 5 BCE, the Pax Romana was well-established following the civil wars of the late Republic. Augustus implemented reforms to the military, provincial administration, and taxation. The empire was at relative peace, with military campaigns focused on frontier consolidation rather than major conquest.
key_figures: Agrippa (military commander, deceased 12 BCE but his legacy shaped policy)

sources.name: Res Gestae Divi Augusti (Augustus's own account)

events.certainty_note: Augustus's reign and general policies are well-documented. The Res Gestae provides contemporary evidence of his rule and achievements.

events.title: Western Han dynasty under Emperor Xuan; stability and cultural flourishing
events.description: Emperor Xuan of Han (r. 74–49 BCE) rules during a period of Han stability and cultural achievement. The Western Han had recovered from earlier crises and was experiencing economic prosperity and military strength. Xuan's reign saw capable administration, military campaigns against the Xiongnu, and cultural patronage. This period represents the height of Han power before the dynasty's gradual decline.
key_figures: Huo Guang (regent and political figure, died 68 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, supplemented by Han Shu (Book of Han)
Archaeological evidence from Han tombs and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Emperor Xuan's reign is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The dating is secure within the proleptic Gregorian calendar conversion.

events.title: Satavahana dynasty expansion in the Deccan; trade network development
events.description: The Satavahana dynasty, ruling in the Deccan region of India, continues its expansion and consolidation during this period. The Satavahanas were developing extensive trade networks, particularly maritime trade with the Roman Empire and Southeast Asia. This era saw the flourishing of Satavahana art, architecture, and commerce. The dynasty served as a crucial intermediary in Indian Ocean trade networks.
key_figures: Satavahana rulers (specific ruler for 5 BCE uncertain due to chronological disputes)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Satavahana sites (coins, inscriptions, pottery)
Periplus of the Erythraean Sea (Greek merchant account, 1st century CE)

events.certainty_note: Satavahana chronology is disputed among scholars. The dynasty's reign dates vary by ±10–20 years depending on the source. This year falls within the probable period of Satavahana power, but specific events are not year-dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Satavahana rulers remains contentious in modern scholarship, with some scholars placing key figures 10–20 years earlier or later than traditional chronologies. Additionally, while Herod's construction projects are well-attested, the specific dating of individual projects to particular years is often uncertain; later sources may conflate or misdate these activities. The significance of 5 BCE as a turning point in any region is not strongly supported by evidence—it is largely an artifact of the proleptic Gregorian calendar rather than a year of major historical rupture.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 5 BCE is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world (Rome, Judaea, Greece) and China, where written records are abundant. Indian history relies more on archaeological evidence and later compilations, introducing greater uncertainty. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have virtually no written documentation for this year. The surviving sources are often biased toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural history must be inferred from archaeology or later sources. Roman sources tend to emphasize Augustus's achievements; Chinese sources reflect court perspectives and may omit regional variations.

graph_edges.note: Herod's reign continues into subsequent years; his construction projects and policies shaped Judaea's trajectory
Augustus's Rome and Emperor Xuan's Han were the two major superpowers of this era, operating independently but aware of each other through trade networks
Satavahana trade networks were beginning to connect with Roman merchants, facilitating Indian Ocean commerce

era_context: Continuation of the early Pax Romana under Augustus. The Roman Empire is consolidating its territorial gains and administrative structures. In China, Emperor Xuan of the Western Han continues his reign. In Judaea, Herod the Great remains in power as a Roman client. The Satavahana dynasty continues its expansion in India. This period represents relative stability across the major civilizations of Eurasia, with trade networks beginning to intensify.

events.title: Augustus's reign continues; military campaigns on frontiers
events.description: Augustus continues military consolidation of the Roman Empire's frontiers. This period saw ongoing campaigns in various provinces to suppress banditry, secure borders, and maintain order. The empire was not engaged in major conquest but rather in stabilization and administrative integration of previously conquered territories. Military spending and organization were reformed to support the Pax Romana.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's general policies and military organization are well-documented, though specific campaigns in 6 BCE are not individually dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Emperor Xuan of Han; continued stability and prosperity
events.description: Emperor Xuan continues his reign over the Western Han, maintaining the stability and prosperity that characterized his rule. The Han military remained strong, with campaigns against the Xiongnu and other northern peoples. The economy was flourishing, supported by the Silk Road trade networks that were developing during this period. Cultural and artistic achievements continued to mark this era.
events.certainty_note: Emperor Xuan's reign is well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Herod the Great's rule in Judaea; continued construction and consolidation
events.description: Herod I continues his rule as client king of Judaea. His reign was characterized by ambitious building projects, including the renovation of the Second Temple in Jerusalem, the construction of fortifications, and the establishment of new cities. Herod maintained order through a combination of patronage and repression, balancing the interests of the Jewish population with Roman strategic requirements.
events.certainty_note: Herod's reign is well-attested overall, but specific events within individual years lack contemporary documentation.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the general political situation in 6 BCE. However, the significance of this particular year is not marked by any major historical rupture or turning point in surviving sources. It is largely an arbitrary point within broader historical processes.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 6 BCE is dominated by Mediterranean and Chinese sources. Roman sources emphasize Augustus's achievements and policies; Chinese sources reflect court perspectives. Judaea's history is known primarily through Josephus, writing in the 1st century CE, introducing potential distortions and anachronisms. The broader world—Africa, Northern Europe, the Americas, and most of Asia—remains largely undocumented for this specific year. The sources available are biased toward elite political and military history.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Augustus's broader consolidation strategy
Continuation of Emperor Xuan's reign and Han prosperity

era_context: The early Roman Empire under Augustus is firmly established. The Western Han dynasty in China continues under Emperor Xuan. Judaea remains under Herod the Great's rule as a Roman client state. The Satavahana dynasty continues its expansion in India. This period represents the height of stability in the major civilizations of Eurasia, with trade networks becoming increasingly important for economic and cultural exchange.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited written sources for this specific year)

events.title: Augustus's reign; administrative and military consolidation
events.description: Augustus continues his rule over the Roman Empire, maintaining the Pax Romana through careful military organization and administrative reform. The empire was at peace, with military resources focused on frontier security rather than major conquest. Augustus's reign saw the development of a professional military, improved taxation systems, and the integration of diverse provinces into a unified administrative structure.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's reign and general policies are well-documented through contemporary sources.

events.title: Emperor Xuan of Han; military campaigns and cultural patronage
events.description: Emperor Xuan continues his reign, conducting military campaigns against the Xiongnu and other northern peoples to secure the Han frontier. The Han military was at the height of its power during this period. Simultaneously, Xuan was a patron of the arts and literature, supporting scholars and artists. The economy was prosperous, with trade networks expanding along what would later be called the Silk Road.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Han military sites and tombs

events.certainty_note: Emperor Xuan's reign is well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Herod the Great's construction projects in Judaea
events.description: Herod I continues his ambitious building program in Judaea. Major projects included the renovation and expansion of the Second Temple in Jerusalem, the construction of the fortress of Masada, and the establishment of new cities such as Caesarea Maritima. These projects served both to enhance Herod's prestige and to integrate Judaea more fully into the Hellenistic world while maintaining Jewish religious traditions.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Herodian sites (Masada, Caesarea, Jerusalem)

events.certainty_note: Herod's construction projects are well-attested archaeologically, but the specific dating of individual projects to particular years is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. However, the significance of 7 BCE as a distinct historical year is not marked by any major turning point or rupture in surviving sources. The year is largely arbitrary within broader historical processes.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 7 BCE is dominated by Mediterranean and Chinese sources, with a strong bias toward elite political and military history. Roman sources emphasize Augustus's achievements; Chinese sources reflect court perspectives. Judaea's history is known primarily through Josephus, writing centuries later. The broader world remains largely undocumented for this specific year. Archaeological evidence provides some corrective to textual sources, particularly for Herod's building projects.

Continuation of Emperor Xuan's reign
Continuation of Herod's building program

The Satavahana dynasty continues its expansion in India. This period represents a peak of stability and prosperity across the major civilizations of Eurasia, with trade networks becoming increasingly important.

events.title: Augustus's reign; Pax Romana at its height
events.description: Augustus continues his rule over the Roman Empire during a period of relative peace and prosperity. The Pax Romana was firmly established, with military resources focused on frontier security. Augustus implemented administrative reforms, including the reorganization of provinces and the professionalization of the military. The empire was experiencing economic growth, supported by improved infrastructure and trade networks.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's reign and general policies are well-documented through contemporary sources.

events.title: Emperor Xuan of Han; peak of Han power and prosperity
events.description: Emperor Xuan of Han continues his reign during a period of Han prosperity and military strength. The Han had achieved military dominance over the Xiongnu and other northern peoples, securing the frontier. The economy was flourishing, with trade networks expanding. This period represents the height of Han power and cultural achievement, with significant developments in literature, art, and philosophy.
events.certainty_note: Emperor Xuan's reign is well-documented in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Herod the Great's reign; continued building and consolidation
events.description: Herod I continues his rule as client king of Judaea, maintaining stability and conducting ambitious building projects. His reign was characterized by a balance between Jewish religious traditions and Hellenistic culture, with construction projects that served both to enhance his prestige and to integrate Judaea into the broader Hellenistic world. Herod maintained order through a combination of patronage and repression.
events.certainty_note: Herod's reign is well-attested overall, but specific events within individual years lack contemporary documentation.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. However, the significance of 8 BCE as a distinct historical year is not marked by any major turning point in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 8 BCE is dominated by Mediterranean and Chinese sources. Roman sources emphasize Augustus's achievements; Chinese sources reflect court perspectives. Judaea's history is known primarily through Josephus, writing centuries later. The broader world remains largely undocumented for this specific year. Archaeological evidence provides important correctives to textual sources.

The Satavahana dynasty continues its expansion in India. This period represents the height of stability across the major civilizations of Eurasia.
